Hey! I'd be happy to help you write a bash script for that. This is a common pattern for window manager keybindings - switching to an app if it's running, or launching it if it's not.
```
Viewing artifacts created via the Analysis Tool web feature preview isn’t yet supported on mobile.
```

Here's a bash script that does exactly what you need! 

**How it works:**
- Uses `wmctrl` to find windows by their class name
- If the window exists, it activates/switches to it
- If not, it launches the application

**Setup:**

1. Make it executable:
```bash
chmod +x switch-or-launch.sh
```

2. Install `wmctrl` if you don't have it:
```bash
sudo apt install wmctrl  # Debian/Ubuntu
sudo pacman -S wmctrl    # Arch
```

3. In your Openbox `~/.config/openbox/rc.xml`, add keybindings like:
```xml
<keybind key="W-f">
  <action name="Execute">
    <command>/path/to/switch-or-launch.sh firefox firefox</command>
  </action>
</keybind>
```

**To find window class names:**
Run `wmctrl -lx` or `xprop | grep CLASS` and click on a window.

The script will find the last active window of that class (the first one returned by wmctrl) and switch to it, or launch the app if none exists. Let me know if you need any adjustments!

input.title: Openbox Window Switcher Script
input.content:
#!/bin/bash

# Usage: ./switch-or-launch.sh <window_class> <command>
# Example: ./switch-or-launch.sh firefox firefox
# Example: ./switch-or-launch.sh Alacritty alacritty

if [ $# -lt 2 ]; then
    echo "Usage: $0 <window_class> <command>"
    exit 1
fi

WINDOW_CLASS="$1"
LAUNCH_CMD="${@:2}"

# Find window ID by class name
WINDOW_ID=$(wmctrl -lx | grep -i "$WINDOW_CLASS" | head -1 | awk '{print $1}')

if [ -n "$WINDOW_ID" ]; then
    # Window exists, activate it
    wmctrl -ia "$WINDOW_ID"
else
    # Window doesn't exist, launch the application
    $LAUNCH_CMD &
fi

```
Viewing artifacts created via the Analysis Tool web feature preview isn’t yet supported on mobile.
```

You'll need `xdotool` installed as well:

input.content:
#!/bin/bash

# Usage: ./switch-or-launch.sh <window_class> <command>
# Example: ./switch-or-launch.sh firefox firefox
# Example: ./switch-or-launch.sh Alacritty alacritty

if [ $# -lt 2 ]; then
    echo "Usage: $0 <window_class> <command>"
    exit 1
fi

WINDOW_CLASS="$1"
shift
LAUNCH_CMD="$@"

# Get current active window
ACTIVE_WINDOW=$(xdotool getactivewindow)

# Find all window IDs matching the class, sorted by stacking order (most recent first)
WINDOW_IDS=$(wmctrl -lx | grep -i "$WINDOW_CLASS" | awk '{print $1}')

if [ -n "$WINDOW_IDS" ]; then
    # Get the window stack order (bottom to top)
    STACK_ORDER=$(xprop -root _NET_CLIENT_LIST_STACKING | grep -o '0x[0-9a-f]*')
    
    # Find the topmost window matching our class (last in stack = most recent)
    LAST_ACTIVE=""
    for wid in $STACK_ORDER; do
        # Convert hex to decimal for comparison
        wid_dec=$(printf "%d" $wid)
        for match in $WINDOW_IDS; do
            match_dec=$(printf "%d" $match)
            if [ "$wid_dec" -eq "$match_dec" ]; then
                LAST_ACTIVE=$match
            fi
        done
    done
    
    if [ -n "$LAST_ACTIVE" ]; then
        wmctrl -i -a "$LAST_ACTIVE"
    else
        # Fallback to first match if stack order fails
        wmctrl -i -a $(echo "$WINDOW_IDS" | head -1)
    fi
else
    # Window doesn't exist, launch the application
    eval "$LAUNCH_CMD" &
fi

Now it uses `xprop` to get the actual window stacking order from the X server, which reflects which window was most recently active/focused. The windows are listed bottom-to-top, so the last matching window in the stack is the most recently active one.

You'll need `xdotool` installed as well:
```bash
sudo apt install xdotool  # Debian/Ubuntu
sudo pacman -S xdotool    # Arch
```

This should now properly switch to the last window you had focused for that application!

input.title: Cycle Same Application Windows
input.content:
#!/bin/bash

# Get the currently active window
ACTIVE_WINDOW=$(xdotool getactivewindow)

# Get the window class of the active window
ACTIVE_CLASS=$(xprop -id "$ACTIVE_WINDOW" WM_CLASS | cut -d'"' -f4)

if [ -z "$ACTIVE_CLASS" ]; then
    exit 1
fi

# Get all window IDs matching the same class
MATCHING_WINDOWS=$(wmctrl -lx | grep -i "$ACTIVE_CLASS" | awk '{print $1}')

# Count how many windows match
WINDOW_COUNT=$(echo "$MATCHING_WINDOWS" | wc -l)

# If only one window, do nothing
if [ "$WINDOW_COUNT" -le 1 ]; then
    exit 0
fi

# Get window stack order (bottom to top)
STACK_ORDER=$(xprop -root _NET_CLIENT_LIST_STACKING | grep -o '0x[0-9a-f]*')

# Build array of matching windows in stack order
ORDERED_WINDOWS=()
for wid in $STACK_ORDER; do
    wid_dec=$(printf "%d" $wid)
    for match in $MATCHING_WINDOWS; do
        match_dec=$(printf "%d" $match)
        if [ "$wid_dec" -eq "$match_dec" ]; then
            ORDERED_WINDOWS+=($match)
        fi
    done
done

# Find current window position in the ordered list
CURRENT_INDEX=-1
for i in "${!ORDERED_WINDOWS[@]}"; do
    if [ "$(printf "%d" ${ORDERED_WINDOWS[$i]})" -eq "$(printf "%d" $ACTIVE_WINDOW)" ]; then
        CURRENT_INDEX=$i
        break
    fi
done

# Calculate next window index (wrap around)
NEXT_INDEX=$(( (CURRENT_INDEX + 1) % ${#ORDERED_WINDOWS[@]} ))
NEXT_WINDOW=${ORDERED_WINDOWS[$NEXT_INDEX]}

# Activate the next window
wmctrl -i -a "$NEXT_WINDOW"

input.title: Openbox Window Switcher with Title Match
input.content:
#!/bin/bash

# Usage: ./switch-or-launch-title.sh <window_class> <title_pattern> <command>
# Example: ./switch-or-launch-title.sh pcmanfm-qt Downloads "pcmanfm-qt ~/Downloads"
# Example: ./switch-or-launch-title.sh firefox Gmail "firefox https://gmail.com"

if [ $# -lt 3 ]; then
    echo "Usage: $0 <window_class> <title_pattern> <command>"
    exit 1
fi

WINDOW_CLASS="$1"
TITLE_PATTERN="$2"
shift 2
LAUNCH_CMD="$@"

# Find all window IDs matching the class, excluding desktop windows
WINDOW_IDS=$(wmctrl -lx | grep -i "$WINDOW_CLASS" | grep -v ' -1 ' | awk '{print $1}')

if [ -n "$WINDOW_IDS" ]; then
    # Check each window for title match
    MATCHING_WINDOW=""
    for wid in $WINDOW_IDS; do
        WINDOW_TITLE=$(xdotool getwindowname "$wid" 2>/dev/null)
        if echo "$WINDOW_TITLE" | grep -qi "$TITLE_PATTERN"; then
            MATCHING_WINDOW="$wid"
            break
        fi
    done
    
    if [ -n "$MATCHING_WINDOW" ]; then
        # Found a window with matching title
        wmctrl -i -a "$MATCHING_WINDOW"
        exit 0
    fi
fi

# No matching window found, launch the application
eval "$LAUNCH_CMD" &

# Wait for window to appear with matching title (up to 3 seconds)
for i in {1..30}; do
    sleep 0.1
    WINDOW_IDS=$(wmctrl -lx | grep -i "$WINDOW_CLASS" | grep -v ' -1 ' | awk '{print $1}')
    for wid in $WINDOW_IDS; do
        WINDOW_TITLE=$(xdotool getwindowname "$wid" 2>/dev/null)
        if echo "$WINDOW_TITLE" | grep -qi "$TITLE_PATTERN"; then
            wmctrl -i -a "$wid"
            exit 0
        fi
    done
done

In Openbox, the command to show the desktop (minimize all windows) is:

```xml
<keybind key="W-d">
  <action name="ToggleShowDesktop"/>
</keybind>
```

This will toggle between showing the desktop and restoring the windows. Press it once to show desktop, press it again to bring windows back.

If you want separate keybindings for showing and hiding:

```xml
<!-- Show desktop -->
<keybind key="W-d">
  <action name="ShowDesktop"/>
</keybind>

<!-- Unshow desktop (restore windows) -->
<keybind key="W-S-d">
  <action name="Unshowdesktop"/>
</keybind>
```

The `ToggleShowDesktop` action is usually what most people prefer since it works like a toggle switch!

content.text:
Interesting challenge! Openbox doesn't handle desktop backgrounds itself - that's usually done by a separate tool. The tricky part is detecting when you switch desktops and changing the background accordingly.

Here's a solution using `feh` (for wallpapers) or `hsetroot` (for solid colors) with a script that monitors desktop changes:

input.title: Desktop Background Color Changer
input.content:
#!/bin/bash

# Desktop background colors (add more as needed)
declare -A COLORS
COLORS[0]="#000000"  # Desktop 1 - Black
COLORS[1]="#404040"  # Desktop 2 - Grey
COLORS[2]="#FFFFFF"  # Desktop 3 - White
COLORS[3]="#0000FF"  # Desktop 4 - Blue
COLORS[4]="#FF0000"  # Desktop 5 - Red
COLORS[5]="#FFFF00"  # Desktop 6 - Yellow
COLORS[6]="#800080"  # Desktop 7 - Purple
COLORS[7]="#00FF00"  # Desktop 8 - Green
COLORS[8]="#FFA500"  # Desktop 9 - Orange

# Function to set background color
set_background() {
    local desktop=$1
    local color=${COLORS[$desktop]}
    
    if [ -n "$color" ]; then
        # Use hsetroot for solid colors (install with: sudo apt install hsetroot)
        hsetroot -solid "$color" 2>/dev/null
        
        # Alternative: use xsetroot (built-in but limited colors)
        # xsetroot -solid "$color"
    fi
}

# Get current desktop on startup and set its background
CURRENT_DESKTOP=$(xprop -root _NET_CURRENT_DESKTOP | awk '{print $3}')
set_background "$CURRENT_DESKTOP"

# Monitor desktop changes
xprop -root -spy _NET_CURRENT_DESKTOP | while read -r line; do
    DESKTOP=$(echo "$line" | awk '{print $3}')
    set_background "$DESKTOP"
done

```bash
sudo pacman -S hsetroot    # Arch
```


3. Add to your Openbox autostart (`~/.config/openbox/autostart`):
```bash

```

4. Customize the colors in the script's `COLORS` array to match your desktop count and preferences.

The script monitors the `_NET_CURRENT_DESKTOP` property and instantly changes the background color when you switch desktops. The `xprop -spy` keeps watching for changes in real-time.

**Alternative**: If `hsetroot` doesn't work well, you could use `feh` with solid color image files, or `xsetroot` (though it has more limited color support). Let me know if you need adjustments!

content.text:
You want a 10px gap around the edges when tiling windows. Unfortunately, Openbox's native actions don't support pixel offsets easily. The best way is to use `wmctrl` or `xdotool` in a script.

Let me create a script for window tiling with gaps:

input.title: Window Tiling with Gaps
input.content:
#!/bin/bash

# Usage: tile-window.sh <position> [gap]
# position: left, right, top, bottom, topleft, topright, bottomleft, bottomright
# gap: pixel gap around edges (default: 10)

POSITION="$1"
GAP="${2:-10}"

# Get screen dimensions
SCREEN_INFO=$(xrandr --current | grep '*' | head -1 | awk '{print $1}')
SCREEN_WIDTH=$(echo $SCREEN_INFO | cut -d'x' -f1)
SCREEN_HEIGHT=$(echo $SCREEN_INFO | cut -d'x' -f2)

# Get active window
WINDOW=$(xdotool getactivewindow)

# Calculate dimensions with gaps
case "$POSITION" in
    left)
        X=$GAP
        Y=$GAP
        WIDTH=$((SCREEN_WIDTH / 2 - GAP - GAP / 2))
        HEIGHT=$((SCREEN_HEIGHT - GAP * 2))
        ;;
    right)
        X=$((SCREEN_WIDTH / 2 + GAP / 2))
        Y=$GAP
        WIDTH=$((SCREEN_WIDTH / 2 - GAP - GAP / 2))
        HEIGHT=$((SCREEN_HEIGHT - GAP * 2))
        ;;
    top)
        X=$GAP
        Y=$GAP
        WIDTH=$((SCREEN_WIDTH - GAP * 2))
        HEIGHT=$((SCREEN_HEIGHT / 2 - GAP - GAP / 2))
        ;;
    bottom)
        X=$GAP
        Y=$((SCREEN_HEIGHT / 2 + GAP / 2))
        WIDTH=$((SCREEN_WIDTH - GAP * 2))
        HEIGHT=$((SCREEN_HEIGHT / 2 - GAP - GAP / 2))
        ;;
    topleft)
        X=$GAP
        Y=$GAP
        WIDTH=$((SCREEN_WIDTH / 2 - GAP - GAP / 2))
        HEIGHT=$((SCREEN_HEIGHT / 2 - GAP - GAP / 2))
        ;;
    topright)
        X=$((SCREEN_WIDTH / 2 + GAP / 2))
        Y=$GAP
        WIDTH=$((SCREEN_WIDTH / 2 - GAP - GAP / 2))
        HEIGHT=$((SCREEN_HEIGHT / 2 - GAP - GAP / 2))
        ;;
    bottomleft)
        X=$GAP
        Y=$((SCREEN_HEIGHT / 2 + GAP / 2))
        WIDTH=$((SCREEN_WIDTH / 2 - GAP - GAP / 2))
        HEIGHT=$((SCREEN_HEIGHT / 2 - GAP - GAP / 2))
        ;;
    bottomright)
        X=$((SCREEN_WIDTH / 2 + GAP / 2))
        Y=$((SCREEN_HEIGHT / 2 + GAP / 2))
        WIDTH=$((SCREEN_WIDTH / 2 - GAP - GAP / 2))
        HEIGHT=$((SCREEN_HEIGHT / 2 - GAP - GAP / 2))
        ;;
    *)
        echo "Unknown position: $POSITION"
        echo "Valid positions: left, right, top, bottom, topleft, topright, bottomleft, bottomright"
        exit 1
        ;;
esac

# Unmaximize window first
wmctrl -ir "$WINDOW" -b remove,maximized_vert,maximized_horz

# Move and resize window
xdotool windowmove "$WINDOW" $X $Y
xdotool windowsize "$WINDOW" $WIDTH $HEIGHT

**From within a Vim document (temporary for that session):**
```vim
:set shiftwidth=1
```

**Set it globally in your vimrc (permanent):**

Edit `~/.vimrc` (or `~/.config/nvim/init.vim` for Neovim) and add:
```vim
set shiftwidth=1
set tabstop=1
set softtabstop=1
set expandtab
```

**Set it for the current file only (modeline):**

Add this as a comment at the top or bottom of your file:
```
# vim: shiftwidth=1 tabstop=1 expandtab
```
or
```
// vim: shiftwidth=1 tabstop=1 expandtab
```

**Quick reference for common settings:**
- `shiftwidth` - spaces for autoindent (>>, <<)
- `tabstop` - how many spaces a tab character displays as
- `softtabstop` - spaces inserted when you press Tab
- `expandtab` - convert tabs to spaces

The vimrc approach is best for a global default. Reload it with `:source ~/.vimrc` or restart Vim.

Let's troubleshoot the autostart. Openbox has two places for autostart files:

1. **~/.config/openbox/autostart** (user-specific, preferred)
2. **/etc/xdg/openbox/autostart** (system-wide)

**Check if the file exists and is executable:**
```bash
ls -la ~/.config/openbox/autostart
```

**The autostart file should:**
- Be executable: `chmod +x ~/.config/openbox/autostart`
- Have a shebang at the top: `#!/bin/bash`
- End commands with `&` to run in background

**Example autostart file:**
```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Desktop color changer
~/.local/bin/desktop-color-changer.sh &

# Any other startup programs
# lxqt-panel &
# picom &
```

**Debug what's running:**


```ini
[Desktop Entry]
```

Aha! The problem is **Vim is capturing the Ctrl+number keys** in your original terminal tabs!

In Vim, Ctrl+] (and some other Ctrl combinations) are tag-related commands, which is why you're seeing the tag errors.

**Quick fix - from within Vim:**
```vim
:unmap <C-4>
:unmap <C-5>
:unmap <C-6>
:unmap <C-7>
:unmap <C-8>
:unmap <C-9>
:unmap <C-0>
```

**Better fix - add to your ~/.vimrc to prevent this:**
```vim
" Don't let Vim capture Ctrl+number keys
for i in range(1, 9)
  execute 'nnoremap <C-' . i . '> <Nop>'
  execute 'inoremap <C-' . i . '> <Nop>'
endfor
nnoremap <C-0> <Nop>
inoremap <C-0> <Nop>
```

Or just **use Ctrl+PageUp/PageDown** to switch tabs when you're in Vim - that's the workaround!

The reason Ctrl+1-3 work is because those particular key combos aren't mapped by default in Vim, but Ctrl+4+ might have some default bindings or plugins interfering.

```vim
" Don't let Vim capture Ctrl+number keys

```

Not easily without closing them, but you can try:

**For each Vim instance, run this command:**
```vim
:source ~/.vimrc
```

**Or to do it in one shot from the terminal:**
```bash
# Send the source command to all running Vim instances
for server in $(vim --serverlist 2>/dev/null); do
  vim --servername "$server" --remote-send ':source ~/.vimrc<CR>'
done
```

But this only works if Vim was started with server support (which most distros don't enable by default).

**Realistically, the easiest approach:**
1. Save your work in each Vim instance (`:wa`)
2. Note the files you have open
3. Close and reopen Vim

Or just remember to use **Ctrl+PageUp/PageDown** in your current sessions, and the new vimrc will apply to new Vim instances going forward.

**Pro tip for future:** Use tmux or screen so you can detach/reattach without losing your session!

**Quick tmux crash course:**

**Starting tmux:**
```bash
tmux                    # Start new session
tmux new -s mysession   # Start with a name
```

**Key bindings (prefix is Ctrl+b, then release, then next key):**
```bash
Ctrl+b c        # Create new window (like a tab)
Ctrl+b 0-9      # Switch to window 0-9
Ctrl+b n        # Next window
Ctrl+b p        # Previous window
Ctrl+b ,        # Rename window

Ctrl+b %        # Split vertically (side-by-side)
Ctrl+b "        # Split horizontally (top-bottom)
Ctrl+b arrow    # Navigate between panes

Ctrl+b d        # Detach (session keeps running!)
Ctrl+b x        # Kill current pane/window
```

**Outside tmux:**
```bash
tmux ls                 # List sessions
tmux attach             # Attach to last session
tmux attach -t name     # Attach to specific session
tmux kill-session -t name  # Kill a session
```

**The magic:** If you get disconnected or close your terminal, just run `tmux attach` and everything is exactly as you left it!

**Pro setup for Alaska tent coding:**
```bash
# Start a named session
tmux new -s work

# Now all your vim, terminals, etc stay alive even if connection drops
# Just: tmux attach -s work
```

input.title: Tmux Configuration
input.content:
# ~/.tmux.conf
# Tmux configuration for comfortable terminal work

# Change prefix from Ctrl+b to Ctrl+a (easier to reach)
unbind C-b
set-option -g prefix C-a
bind-key C-a send-prefix

# Enable mouse support (scroll, click panes, resize)
set -g mouse on

# Start window numbering at 1 instead of 0
set -g base-index 1
set -g pane-base-index 1

# Renumber windows when one is closed
set -g renumber-windows on

# Increase scrollback buffer
set -g history-limit 10000

# Split panes using | and - (more intuitive)
bind | split-window -h
bind - split-window -v
unbind '"'
unbind %

# Switch panes using Alt+arrow without prefix
bind -n M-Left select-pane -L
bind -n M-Right select-pane -R
bind -n M-Up select-pane -U
bind -n M-Down select-pane -D

# Switch windows with Shift+arrow
bind -n S-Left previous-window
bind -n S-Right next-window

# Reload config with prefix+r
bind r source-file ~/.tmux.conf \; display "Config reloaded!"

# Don't auto-rename windows
set-option -g allow-rename off

# Vi mode for copy mode
set-window-option -g mode-keys vi
bind-key -T copy-mode-vi v send-keys -X begin-selection
bind-key -T copy-mode-vi y send-keys -X copy-selection-and-cancel

# Status bar styling
set -g status-position bottom
set -g status-bg colour234
set -g status-fg colour137
set -g status-left ''
set -g status-right '#[fg=colour233,bg=colour241,bold] %m/%d #[fg=colour233,bg=colour245,bold] %H:%M '
set -g status-right-length 50
set -g status-left-length 20

# Window status styling
setw -g window-status-current-format ' #I#[fg=colour250]:#[fg=colour255]#W#[fg=colour50]#F '
setw -g window-status-format ' #I#[fg=colour237]:#[fg=colour250]#W#[fg=colour244]#F '

# Active pane border
set -g pane-active-border-style fg=colour208

# Message styling
set -g message-style fg=colour232,bg=colour166,bold

# Enable 256 color support
set -g default-terminal "screen-256color"

# Reduce escape time (helps with Vim responsiveness)
set -s escape-time 0

**Your goal is clear:** Session persistence across reboots with project-based desktops. Let me break this into phases:

## Phase 1: Quick & Dirty (Get you working NOW)

**Tmux handles your terminal sessions:**
- All your vim files, shell sessions stay alive
- Survives terminal crashes, but NOT reboots
- For reboot persistence, we need to save/restore tmux sessions

**For window positions & apps across desktops, we need a session manager.**

Let me create a simple session save/restore script:

input.title: Session Save/Restore Manager
input.content:
#!/bin/bash
# Session manager for Openbox
# Usage: session-manager.sh save|restore [session-name]

SESSION_DIR="$HOME/.local/share/openbox-sessions"
mkdir -p "$SESSION_DIR"

SESSION_NAME="${2:-default}"
SESSION_FILE="$SESSION_DIR/${SESSION_NAME}.session"

save_session() {
    echo "Saving session: $SESSION_NAME"
    
    # Save current desktop
    CURRENT_DESKTOP=$(xprop -root _NET_CURRENT_DESKTOP | awk '{print $3}')
    echo "CURRENT_DESKTOP=$CURRENT_DESKTOP" > "$SESSION_FILE"
    
    # Save all windows with their properties
    wmctrl -lGpx | while read -r line; do
        WIN_ID=$(echo "$line" | awk '{print $1}')
        DESKTOP=$(echo "$line" | awk '{print $2}')
        PID=$(echo "$line" | awk '{print $3}')
        X=$(echo "$line" | awk '{print $4}')
        Y=$(echo "$line" | awk '{print $5}')
        WIDTH=$(echo "$line" | awk '{print $6}')
        HEIGHT=$(echo "$line" | awk '{print $7}')
        CLASS=$(echo "$line" | awk '{print $8}')
        TITLE=$(echo "$line" | cut -d' ' -f9-)
        
        # Get the command that launched this window
        CMD=$(ps -p $PID -o command= 2>/dev/null)
        
        # Skip desktop windows
        if [ "$DESKTOP" = "-1" ]; then
            continue
        fi
        
        echo "WINDOW|$DESKTOP|$X|$Y|$WIDTH|$HEIGHT|$CLASS|$CMD" >> "$SESSION_FILE"
    done
    
    # Save tmux sessions
    if command -v tmux &> /dev/null; then
        tmux list-sessions -F "TMUX_SESSION|#{session_name}" >> "$SESSION_FILE" 2>/dev/null
    fi
    
    echo "Session saved to: $SESSION_FILE"
    echo "Windows saved: $(grep -c '^WINDOW' "$SESSION_FILE")"
}

restore_session() {
    if [ ! -f "$SESSION_FILE" ]; then
        echo "Session file not found: $SESSION_FILE"
        exit 1
    fi
    
    echo "Restoring session: $SESSION_NAME"
    
    # Read and restore windows
    while IFS='|' read -r TYPE DESKTOP X Y WIDTH HEIGHT CLASS CMD; do
        if [ "$TYPE" = "WINDOW" ]; then
            echo "Launching: $CMD on desktop $DESKTOP"
            
            # Launch the application
            eval "$CMD" &
            LAUNCH_PID=$!
            
            # Wait a moment for window to appear
            sleep 1
            
            # Find the new window and position it
            NEW_WIN=$(wmctrl -lp | grep " $LAUNCH_PID " | awk '{print $1}' | head -1)
            if [ -n "$NEW_WIN" ]; then
                # Move to correct desktop
                wmctrl -i -r "$NEW_WIN" -t "$DESKTOP"
                
                # Position and resize
                xdotool windowmove "$NEW_WIN" "$X" "$Y"
                xdotool windowsize "$NEW_WIN" "$WIDTH" "$HEIGHT"
            fi
        elif [ "$TYPE" = "CURRENT_DESKTOP" ]; then
            # Will switch to this desktop at the end
            RESTORE_DESKTOP=$DESKTOP
        fi
    done < "$SESSION_FILE"
    
    # Switch to the desktop that was active when saved
    if [ -n "$RESTORE_DESKTOP" ]; then
        wmctrl -s "$RESTORE_DESKTOP"
    fi
    
    echo "Session restored!"
}

list_sessions() {
    echo "Available sessions:"
    ls -1 "$SESSION_DIR"/*.session 2>/dev/null | while read -r file; do
        basename "$file" .session
        echo "  Windows: $(grep -c '^WINDOW' "$file")"
    done
}

case "$1" in
    save)
        save_session
        ;;
    restore)
        restore_session
        ;;
    list)
        list_sessions
        ;;
    *)
        echo "Usage: $0 {save|restore|list} [session-name]"
        echo "Examples:"
        echo "  $0 save myproject       # Save current session as 'myproject'"
        echo "  $0 restore myproject    # Restore 'myproject' session"
        echo "  $0 list                 # List all saved sessions"
        exit 1
        ;;
esac
